narration_segments.text: It starts in the chest. Not in the mind. The mind comes second, scrambling to explain what the chest already knows. A tightening. A compression behind the sternum, as though someone pressed a fist into the space between the lungs and held it there. The throat narrows. The jaw locks. The shoulders climb toward the ears as though bracing for an impact that never arrives. This is anxiety arriving. Not as a thought. As a physical event. The nervous system fires before language forms. Adrenaline floods the bloodstream in under half a second.

Her name is Mara. Not the Buddhist Mara, the tempter — though the resonance is intentional. This Mara is thirty-four years old. She works as a project coordinator at a mid-sized architecture firm in a city that gets rain nine months of the year. She is competent. She is organized. She arrives early and leaves late and her inbox is the cleanest in the office. And every Sunday evening, starting around six, her chest begins to tighten. Mara sits on the edge of her bed. Her hands grip the duvet. Her breath becomes shallow, pulled into the upper chest where it flutters like a trapped bird beating against glass. She knows, intellectually, that Monday will be fine. It is always fine. Nothing catastrophic has happened at work. Her reviews are strong. Her colleagues respect her. She has never been reprimanded, never been called out, never been humiliated in a meeting. None of that matters. The body has its own calendar. The body has its own logic. And the body's logic says: the week is approaching. The week is an unpredictable territory. The week contains rooms full of people whose reactions cannot be controlled. Mara's pattern begins in childhood.

Everything in this chapter is designed to be done seated, standing, or lying down. No special equipment. No special room. No perfect silence. The body is the only instrument required. The breath is the only technology. PRACTICE ONE: THE THREE-BREATH RESET This is the foundational practice. It takes less than sixty seconds and it works by speaking the language the vagus nerve understands — the language of rhythm, pressure, and time. Sit or stand. Place both feet on the ground. Feel the weight of the body pressing downward. Gravity is a fact. The ground is a fact. Let both be anchors. Notice the pressure of the floor under the soles. Notice the weight of the body in the chair. These are not ideas. These are sensations. Start there. First breath. Inhale through the nose for a count of four. Let the belly expand. Not the chest — the belly.

Here is the compressed teaching. Hold it the way a hand holds a warm stone — firm, close to the body, felt rather than analyzed. Anxiety is not a flaw in the system. It is a fire alarm wired to the wrong room. The smoke it detects is real — but the fire happened long ago, in a different building, and the building no longer stands. The nervous system cannot tell time. It encodes threat as a permanent present tense.
